learning_objectives: Define psychological testing and differentiate tests from other assessment methods
Trace the historical development of psychological testing
Describe the Standards for Educational and Psychological Testing

lecture_notes.introduction: Psychological testing is one of the most distinctive contributions of psychology to society. Tests are used in education, clinical settings, employment, and forensics. Understanding their construction, psychometric properties, and appropriate use is essential.
key_concepts: Definition: standardized procedure for sampling behavior and describing it with scores
History: Galton, Binet-Simon, Army Alpha/Beta, MMPI
Standards for Educational and Psychological Testing (AERA/APA/NCME)
Types: achievement, aptitude, personality, neuropsychological
Ethical use: informed consent, confidentiality, cultural fairness
lecture_notes.demonstration: Compare a standardized test item to a casual quiz item: identify differences in construction quality
setup_requirements: Sample test items from different domains

review.value: Administering and scoring a test under uniform conditions so that results can be compared across individuals and occasions
Performance of a reference group that provides the context for interpreting individual scores; must be representative and current

learning_objectives: Define reliability as consistency of measurement
Calculate and interpret test-retest, internal consistency, and inter-rater reliability
Explain the standard error of measurement and its clinical applications

lecture_notes.introduction: Reliability is the foundation of measurement quality. An unreliable test cannot be valid, because random error obscures the true score.
key_concepts: Classical test theory: observed score = true score + error
Test-retest reliability: stability over time
Internal consistency: Cronbach's alpha, split-half reliability
Inter-rater reliability: Cohen's kappa, intraclass correlation
Standard error of measurement: SEM = SD * sqrt(1 - r); confidence intervals around scores
lecture_notes.demonstration: Calculate Cronbach's alpha and SEM for a 10-item anxiety scale using provided data

review.value: Measure of internal consistency; alpha ≥ .70 acceptable for research, ≥ .90 recommended for individual clinical decisions
Estimates the range within which the true score likely falls; smaller SEM = more precise measurement. A score of 100 with SEM = 3 means 95% CI is approximately 94-106

learning_objectives: Explain validity as a unitary concept with multiple sources of evidence
Describe content, criterion-related, and construct validity evidence
Evaluate validity evidence for a published psychological test

lecture_notes.introduction: Validity is the most important property of a test. It addresses whether the test measures what it claims to measure and whether inferences drawn from scores are justified.
key_concepts: Validity as a unitary concept: not types but sources of evidence
Content validity: expert judgment, test blueprint coverage
Criterion-related validity: predictive and concurrent designs
Construct validity: convergent and discriminant evidence (MTMM matrix)
Consequential validity: social consequences of test use and interpretation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Evaluate the validity evidence for the Beck Depression Inventory-II using published technical manual data

review.value: A test should correlate highly with measures of the same construct (convergent) and weakly with measures of different constructs (discriminant)
Campbell & Fiske's multitrait-multimethod matrix: examines convergent and discriminant validity across multiple traits measured by multiple methods

learning_objectives: Describe the structure and interpretation of the WAIS-IV
Evaluate the g-factor theory vs. multiple intelligences approaches
Discuss the Flynn effect, group differences, and the heritability-environment debate

lecture_notes.introduction: Intelligence testing is psychology's most controversial and most practically impactful assessment domain. Understanding both the technology and the debates is essential for responsible use.
key_concepts: WAIS-IV structure: Verbal Comprehension, Perceptual Reasoning, Working Memory, Processing Speed
Full Scale IQ: mean = 100, SD = 15; deviation IQ model
g-factor (Spearman) vs. Cattell-Horn-Carroll (CHC) theory
Flynn effect: IQ scores have risen ~3 points per decade; implications for norming
Group differences: socioeconomic, educational, and cultural factors; stereotype threat
lecture_notes.demonstration: Practice interpreting a WAIS-IV profile: identify strengths, weaknesses, and significant discrepancies

review.value: Cattell-Horn-Carroll: intelligence is hierarchical with g at the top, broad abilities (fluid reasoning, crystallized knowledge, processing speed) in the middle, and narrow abilities at the bottom
IQ scores have risen approximately 3 points per decade across the 20th century; means that norms must be updated regularly to avoid score inflation

class_schedule.topic: Personality Assessment: Objective and Projective Methods
learning_objectives: Compare the MMPI-3, NEO-PI-3, and PAI as objective personality instruments
Evaluate the psychometric evidence for the Rorschach (R-PAS) and TAT
Discuss the incremental validity of personality assessment in clinical decision-making

lecture_notes.introduction: Personality assessment is central to clinical psychology. The debate between objective and projective methods has driven improvements in both approaches.
key_concepts: MMPI-3: validity scales, restructured clinical scales, substantive scales
NEO-PI-3: Big Five assessment for normal-range personality
PAI: clinical assessment with response inconsistency and defensiveness scales
Rorschach Performance Assessment System (R-PAS): standardized coding, normative reference
Incremental validity: does the test add information beyond what is already available?
lecture_notes.demonstration: Score and interpret a sample MMPI-3 profile including validity scale analysis

review.value: MMPI scales detecting response distortion: Cannot Say (CNS), Infrequent Responses (F), Lie (L), Correction (K); essential for interpreting clinical scales
Whether a test improves prediction beyond a simpler or cheaper method; Rorschach shows incremental validity for some constructs (thought disorder, implicit dependency) but not others

learning_objectives: Describe the purpose and components of a neuropsychological assessment battery
Explain test accommodation and fairness for diverse populations
Discuss emerging trends: computerized adaptive testing, ecological momentary assessment

lecture_notes.introduction: Specialized assessment domains extend testing beyond personality and intelligence to brain function, forensic questions, and real-time behavioral monitoring.
key_concepts: Neuropsychological assessment: Halstead-Reitan vs. flexible battery approach
Key neuropsych tests: Trail Making, Wisconsin Card Sort, Boston Naming, Rey Complex Figure
Test accommodations for disability: extended time, alternative format, assistive technology
Computerized adaptive testing (CAT): item response theory, tailored difficulty
Ecological momentary assessment: real-time data collection in natural environments
lecture_notes.demonstration: Administer and interpret Trail Making Test (Parts A and B) and discuss what cognitive functions each assesses

review.value: Part A assesses psychomotor speed and visual scanning; Part B adds set-shifting and cognitive flexibility. The B-A difference isolates executive function
Uses item response theory to select items based on previous answers, converging on the examinee's ability level with fewer items and greater precision

questions.question: A clinical psychologist reports that a new anxiety test has a Cronbach's alpha of .82 and a test-retest reliability of .65 over 6 months. If this test is to be used for individual treatment decisions, evaluate whether each reliability coefficient is adequate and explain the discrepancy.
questions.answer: Internal consistency (alpha = .82) is adequate for group research (>.70) but below the recommended threshold for individual clinical decisions (>.90). The test-retest reliability (.65) is concerning for a trait measure -- anxiety assessed as a stable disposition should show higher temporal stability. The discrepancy likely reflects that the test is partly measuring state anxiety (which fluctuates), not just trait anxiety. For individual treatment decisions, neither coefficient is adequate. The psychologist should consider a more reliable instrument or supplement with additional measures.

rubric.key: Evaluates test-retest against expectations for trait measures

questions.question: Explain what a multitrait-multimethod (MTMM) matrix evaluates and describe what pattern of correlations would demonstrate good construct validity.
questions.answer: An MTMM matrix evaluates construct validity by measuring multiple traits using multiple methods and examining the pattern of correlations. Good construct validity requires: (1) Convergent validity: high correlations between the same trait measured by different methods (e.g., anxiety measured by self-report and clinician rating should correlate highly). (2) Discriminant validity: low correlations between different traits measured by the same method (e.g., self-reported anxiety and self-reported extraversion should not correlate highly). (3) Method variance control: same-method correlations between different traits should be lower than different-method correlations for the same trait. If method variance is too high, the test may be measuring the method (e.g., acquiescence) rather than the trait.

rubric.key: Correctly describes MTMM purpose and structure
Explains discriminant validity pattern and method variance

questions.question: A child obtains an FSIQ of 68 on the WISC-V. The school psychologist is considering a diagnosis of intellectual disability. What additional information beyond the IQ score is required, and why might the score alone be misleading?
questions.answer: Intellectual disability requires deficits in BOTH intellectual functioning AND adaptive behavior (conceptual, social, practical skills). An IQ of 68 is within the range (below ~70-75), but the score alone is insufficient because: (1) The SEM (~3-5 points for WISC-V FSIQ) means the true score could be above 70. (2) The child's adaptive functioning must be independently assessed (e.g., Vineland-3). A child with an IQ of 68 but adequate adaptive skills does not meet criteria. (3) Cultural and linguistic factors may have lowered the score (e.g., English language learner, unfamiliarity with test content). (4) Profile analysis may reveal significant strengths masked by the composite score.

rubric.key: States requirement for adaptive behavior assessment
Notes SEM and confidence interval consideration
Identifies cultural/linguistic factors as potential confounds

questions.question: Why are validity scales essential when interpreting the MMPI-3? Describe what two specific validity scales measure and how elevated scores affect interpretation.
questions.answer: Validity scales are essential because the MMPI-3 relies on self-report, which is susceptible to response distortion (faking good, faking bad, random responding). Two scales: (1) F (Infrequent Responses): detects over-reporting of symptoms. Items are endorsed by less than 10% of the normative sample. Elevated F suggests the person is exaggerating pathology (cry for help, malingering, or confusion). Clinical scales will be artificially elevated and cannot be interpreted at face value. (2) L (Uncommon Virtues): detects under-reporting or faking good. Items describe minor flaws that most people endorse. Elevated L suggests the person is presenting an unrealistically positive self-image. Clinical scales will be artificially suppressed, and genuine pathology may be hidden.

rubric.key: Explains why validity scales are necessary in self-report
Explains how elevations affect clinical scale interpretation

major_assignments.title: Psychological Test Review and Critique
major_assignments.description: Select a published psychological test and conduct a comprehensive psychometric review following the Standards for Educational and Psychological Testing. Evaluate reliability, validity, norming, fairness, and practical utility. Write a professional test review suitable for publication in a reference work.
objectives: Critically evaluate the psychometric properties of a published test
Apply the Standards for Educational and Psychological Testing
Assess test fairness for diverse populations
deliverables: Overall evaluation with recommendations for use and limitations
Minimum 10 references including the test manual

criteria: Evaluates content, criterion, and construct validity evidence
Evaluates evidence of differential item functioning
Balanced evaluation with strengths and limitations
Thorough reliability analysis with appropriate standards
Comprehensive validity evaluation across multiple evidence sources
Thoughtful fairness analysis for diverse populations

weekly_quizzes.description: Assessment of psychometric concepts and test interpretation

lab_exercises.description: Hands-on test administration, scoring, and interpretation practice

midterm_examination.description: Exam covering reliability, validity, and intelligence testing

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam including personality and neuropsychological assessment

course_policies.attendance: Lab attendance is mandatory for test administration practice. Lecture attendance is strongly recommended.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. Test review papers will not be accepted more than 5 days late.
course_policies.collaboration: Lab partners may practice administration together. All written assignments must be independently authored.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Test security is paramount. Actual test items and protocols must not be shared outside the classroom.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to test publisher websites, university library (Mental Measurements Yearbook), and scoring software.
